does: This paper proposes a new continual learning method for large language models that regularizes in activation space rather than weight space. The approach uses pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to identify monosemantic features and constructs a constrained optimization loss function that explicitly balances stability and plasticity across sequential tasks. The method masks SAE features corresponding to knowledge from previous tasks, preventing their modification during training on new tasks without requiring storage or replay of previous task data. The authors derive this approach from first principles, showing that Elastic Weight Consolidation emerges as a special case of their framework when regularization is applied one-sidedly in weight space.
connects: This work builds on decades of continual learning research aimed at mitigating catastrophic forgetting, extending weight-space regularization methods like Elastic Weight Consolidation that have become standard in the field. It responds to the observation that EWC-style approaches underperform on large language models, connecting to recent insights about polysemanticity in neural networks where individual weights encode multiple unrelated concepts. The paper leverages the emerging field of mechanistic interpretability, specifically Sparse Autoencoders developed for discovering monosemantic features in LLM activations. It also relates to recent work on feature-space regularization and connects to the broader literature on stability-plasticity tradeoffs in neural networks.
enables: This approach opens new directions for understanding and controlling forgetting in large models by operating at the feature level rather than weight level. It enables future work on more interpretable continual learning, where protected knowledge can be explicitly mapped to semantic features rather than implicit weight configurations. The SAE-guided regularization framework could extend to other multi-task and sequential learning scenarios, particularly where model interpretability is valuable. It points toward combining mechanistic interpretability tools with continual learning to achieve better task transfer, and suggests that feature dictionaries learned from pretraining could serve as universal scaffolds for managing knowledge across different sequential learning problems.
